Defendant Uber Technologies, Inc. Demurrer To Complaint For The Purposes Of Tentative Ruling Only. Part 2 Of 2 Parts (302)
Off Calendar Jun-24-2022 Off Calendar

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-24-2022 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. PETER HAKIM, Esq. of Downtown L.A. Law Group (Tel: 213-389-3765) for plaintiff;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Uber Technologies, Inc.'s ("Uber's") demurrer to Plaintiff's fourth cause of action for negligence is sustained with leave to amend.

"[A] principal may be liable for the wrongful conduct of its agent, even if that conduct is criminal, in one of three ways: (1) if the principal directly authorizes ... [the tort or] crime to be committed; (2) if the agent commits the tort in the scope of his employment and in performing service on behalf of the principal, regardless of whether the wrong is authorized or ratified by [the principal], and even if the wrong is criminal; or (3) if the principal ratifies its agent's conduct after the fact." (Doe v. Roman Cath. Archbishop of Los Angeles (2016) 247 Cal.App.4th 953, 969 (internal quotations and citations omitted).) Even if an employer has not authorized an employee's actions, the employer may still be liable for the intentional torts of its employee if there was a causal nexus between the intentional tort and the employee's work. (Lisa M. v. Henry Mayo Newhall Mem'l Hosp. (1995) 12 Cal.4th 291, 297.) "The nexus required for respondeat superior liability [is] that the tort be engendered by or arise from the work That the employment brought tortfeasor and victim together in time and place is not enough the incident leading to injury must be an outgrowth of the employment; the risk of tortious injury must be inherent in the working environment or typical of or broadly incidental to the enterprise [the employer] has undertaken." (Id. at 298.)

Plaintiff fails to state facts sufficient to state a cause of action for negligence against Uber. The Court "treats the demurrer as admitting all material facts properly pleaded. The court does not, however, assume the truth of contentions, deductions or conclusions of law." (Aubry v. Tri-City Hosp. Dist. (1992) 2 Cal.4th 962, 967 (internal citations omitted).) Plaintiff alleges that her Uber driver was acting within the scope of her authority as an agent of Uber when she assaulted Plaintiff. (Complaint, 6.) Plaintiff also alleges that Uber "gave consent to, ratified, and authorized" the driver's intentional torts. (Id.) These allegations are conclusory. Plaintiff is granted leave to allege ultimate facts to state a cause of action for negligence against Uber.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/SRB)
